item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A great read albeit with some oddly phrased sentences

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this tale so much

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
William's writing doesn't sound right to me at all, which is really unfortunate because it's the focus of this whole thing, and that's ultimately why I downvoted.

Someone in a previous comment mentioned the "I was more concerned about trying to calm her down by telling her that maybe they weren't really there than I was about seeing Capone in the mirror" bit and that also bothered me a lot because I had to reread it three times before I realized what that sentence was trying to communicate. It probably also says something that I wasn't completely clear on the timeline until I read the comments here.

I say William's writing didn't sound authentic to me because it sounds like he was trying to write a creepy story rather than
recounting events for his therapist. The tone/style of it isn't at all different from the parts he hadn't written. Maybe it's the perspective as someone else mentioned; the only suggestion I can give is to pay more attention to the voice you give your characters versus your own voice as a writer.

I like the idea of this, just not the execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I might have read or misinterpreted something wrong but is SCP-1471 common knowledge in this tale? If it is then I feel this should probably be in The Broken Masquerade hub

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this look at people outside the Foundation having an experience with Mal0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Imagine teaching this thing to talk. Get home from school, greater by your hell-cat. I wouldn't mind this thing after getting used to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would like to have a companion like capone ;V

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a little confused… How would things have been any different if he hadn't looked at that text?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aww. I feel so sorry for everyone involved, even the MalOs. I bet Cassandra just wanted a friend, she couldn't understand what Sara was saying and that she wanted her to leave, and then she inadvertently brought her to suicide. Perhaps she even understood it was her fault. Perhaps the app is like an interspecies friend request, these beings want to be friends with us but are so different they find it hard to understand us, so they do the best they can to find people who can be their companions. I think I would tell Sara something like that if she was my sister, that we should try to get past their appearance and see that they are different but not evil at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed it. You took an SCP that sort of terrified me when I first read it and turned it into simultaneous horror and wonder. It isn't easy to combine two very different experiences of the same phenomenon into one story and make it comprehensible. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was the only real back and forth in the tale, so fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
“Believe me William; I know many people have reacted the same way. Fear is a more common reaction to the unknown than you’d think.” Dr. Laura Breynz consoled him. These cases weren't exactly common, but they were commonly known. “It’s not even that I'm afraid of him anymore. I mean, he makes me jump when I see him in the morning, I don't think I'll ever get used to that; It's the just the bad connotations he has.” William rebutted. "I know that he does, but it happened two years ago. I know it's hard for you, especially with your shadow, but you need to carry on." Dr. Breynz stated.

Two people are talking in this paragraph. They have a back and forth, in one paragraph. I can't speak for the quality of the rest of the tale as I stopped reading here, because it's too poorly done to trudge through what comes next.

Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I enjoyed the premise of this, I can't help but feel annoyed by some grammatical oversights. The psychiatry session is pretty jumbled, usually when somebody has different characters speaking they create a new paragraph, even if for a few lines. Also some sentences didn't really make sense to me. Like; " I was more concerned about trying to calm her down by telling her that maybe they weren't really there than I was about seeing Capone in the mirror." Are you saying he was trying to calm her down, than suddenly saw Capone in the mirror? So unfortunately I downvoted it.

I will say this though, even though I downvoted this, I admire your "Pitch Haven" series SCP's and look completely forward to the tales that you will make on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the most peaceful things I've ever seen on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not so much a poke at you, but a poke at myself. I find it adorable, and I have no idea why.

Anyway, my only response to that is that I find myself disagreeing. My goal with this was to give a realistic take on a couple of normal people who would find themselves in this situation, while also displaying the MalOs 'intentions', without giving a definite answer of why they do it (outside of 'it's just what they do'). It's kind of like a ‘Meet the MalO’, where you know what it is, but you get to see just what being with a MalO is like, except instead of humor, it's drama/horror/odd cuteness.

I dunno, but either way, thanks for reading!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm proud to say that that feeling was indeed my intention. ^^

The article states that MalOs tend to try and talk to you, but no one has been able to decipher what they're saying. It also states that 'the more you participate, the more MalO will engage you'.

My interpretation from that, is that they are totally capable of learning how to understand languages from you just talking to them after a while, and either come from somewhere with their own native sign-language, or are just 'programed' to know it.

I'm guessing that if someone with William's symptoms tried to teach a MalO sign-language, they'd be at least partially successful. Although, I'm also guessing their intelligence level is somewhere between a dog's and a monkey's.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"…he had to teach it how to wave."

That has interesting implications. The creature has some intelligence, at least as much as a dog or cat, and some form of communication with it is possible. Somebody at the Foundation is definitely going to pick up on that and start experimenting…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It waves back. Horrifically brilliant. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was how I interpreted it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like many first-person pieces here, this suffers from tone consistency problems: the phrase "some bestial abomination" in particular seemed really at odds with the narrator's writing style, and I found the closing sentences a little too histrionic. However, I think there are too few stories showing people carrying on some semblance of normal life after their encounter with the supernatural, and I really like the few glimpses of Capone's behaviour that we do get. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the story could easily be a few years into the future, no?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neh, it shouldn't hurt. 15's still pretty young. I think it's fine as is regarding explanations though. I find myself a little irate when I hear the foundation making fronts with the initials 'SCP' in them though. It's like yelling "ha ha, you can't see me!" while hiding behind a sign that reads 'totally normal stuff over here! Move along!'

I think people can draw their own conclusions. It'll be able to fit their own head cannons that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll never understand the way Sara saw the world, if that was her idea of 'cute'

… This isn't a poke at me is it? Or is my paranoia just flarin up again? (Maybe I need more coffee)

Anyhow, didn't care for it, it seemed merely to expound on the article in prose format. It didn't really change anything or provide a new feeling or perspective to the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, he'd have been thirteen seven years ago. That would be 2006, then. The App Store launched July 2008. I don't think the flip-phone's gonna cover that. Would it hurt much to make him a couple years older when he first got Malo?

If you're going with explanation 2, you should probably slip in that he's going to "Something C-starting-with Psychiatric" or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found that kinda funny. As if to say "in four days, THEY GO APE-SHIT!!!"

Anyway, my intention was that Sara waited for William to move out before she committed suicide, so here's the general timeline:

13 years old: MalO is downloaded
plus 4 days: "Hi guys!"
18 years: William moves out for college
plus 1-7 days: Sara commits suicide
20 years old: present

e: As for the year, I mentioned that William got his MalO on his crappy flip-phone, so that should cover any time-based errors.

As for the foundation, you could interpret this in many ways, two explanations come to mind off the top of my head:

1) The world already knows about anomalous stuff, like in the broken masquerade cannon, and The Foundation was spending their resources elsewhere.
2) This is how they found MalO, and William is going to a foundation front for therapy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the timeline could use some clarification.

Here's what I'm seeing:
13 yrs old: William discovers Malo via Sara
plus 4 days : They freak out
?? William moves out for college
2 ys ago : Sara commits suicide

Namely, I'm thinking that app stores and such are a bit too recent for him to be contemporary with them at 13 unless he's headed for college at 15
(and Sara didn't long outlive the freakout).
2013 -4/5 years for him to reach 17/18 (since if Sara had died before he moved out, he'd have mentioned it), - 2 years for present day gets us 2007-2006. When was the first App Store?

Also, where's the Foundation in all this?

EDIT:Oops, accidentally plus sized that "4 days".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks man. I appreciate it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for pointing those out. Fixed, and adjusted the things I missed to past tense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fantem this is maginficent.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's almost a bit… too much of a reveal, here? And it gets a little muddled around the middle bit, when they're hiding in the bathroom, but I still read the entire thing without feeling bored and rather enjoyed the mundane psychological treatment of the wacky supernatural.

There are some very stilted phrases, though:

One thing I have yet to understand till this day is Sara's definition of any word used to positively describe any given thing.

this sentence makes almost no sense— did you mean "'cute', it wasn't" or "I'll never understand the way Sara saw the world, if that was her idea of 'cute'"

his incisive claws

probably not the modifier you want to use

expressing how scared of it I was

"I told her it was terrifying"

Her pale face was carrying an expression of pure dread.

anything variation that doesn't involve "face was carrying and expression of"

your verb tense also changes up a few times— between "it's been a few hours" and "William was"; is this present or past?

item.title: Author post
item.content:encoded:
A SCP-1471 tale. Original article was written by Lurk D, who I can't thank enough for allowing me to write about it.

I hope you all enjoy. ^_^
